sourceRefs.fallbackText: The vinyl and plush gophers are copyrighted designs; accept no substitutes! But how can you get one? Their natural habitat is near high concentrations of Go programmers, and their worldwide population is growing. They may be purchased from the Google Store , although the supply can be irregular. (These elusive creatures have been spotted in all kinds of plac

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Rob Pike and Andrew Gerrand 24 March 2014
sourceRefs.textQuote: Rob Pike and Andrew Gerrand 24 March 2014

highSignalBlocks.text: Rob Pike and Andrew Gerrand 24 March 2014

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go gopher is an iconic mascot and one of the most distinctive features of the Go project. In this post we’ll talk about its origins, evolution, and behavior.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go gopher is an iconic mascot and one of the most distinctive features of the Go project. In this post we’ll talk about its origins, evolution, and behavior.

highSignalBlocks.text: The Go gopher is an iconic mascot and one of the most distinctive features of the Go project. In this post we’ll talk about its origins, evolution, and behavior.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: About 15 years ago—long before the Go project—the gopher first appeared as a promotion for the WFMU radio station in New Jersey. Renee French was commissioned to design a T-shirt for an annual fundraiser and out came the gopher.
sourceRefs.textQuote: About 15 years ago—long before the Go project—the gopher first appeared as a promotion for the WFMU radio station in New Jersey. Renee French was commissioned to design a T-shirt for an annual fundraiser and out came the

highSignalBlocks.text: About 15 years ago—long before the Go project—the gopher first appeared as a promotion for the WFMU radio station in New Jersey. Renee French was commissioned to design a T-shirt for an annual fundraiser and out came the gopher.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The gopher next made an appearance at Bell Labs, as Bob Flandrena’s avatar in the Bell Labs mail system. Other Renee drawings became avatars for ken, r, rsc, and others. (Of course, Peter Weinberger’s was his own iconic face .)
sourceRefs.textQuote: The gopher next made an appearance at Bell Labs, as Bob Flandrena’s avatar in the Bell Labs mail system. Other Renee drawings became avatars for ken, r, rsc, and others. (Of course, Peter Weinberger’s was his own iconic

highSignalBlocks.text: The gopher next made an appearance at Bell Labs, as Bob Flandrena’s avatar in the Bell Labs mail system. Other Renee drawings became avatars for ken, r, rsc, and others. (Of course, Peter Weinberger’s was his own iconic face .)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another Bell Labs activity led to Renee creating Glenda , the Plan 9 mascot, a distant cousin of the WFMU gopher.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another Bell Labs activity led to Renee creating Glenda , the Plan 9 mascot, a distant cousin of the WFMU gopher.

highSignalBlocks.text: Another Bell Labs activity led to Renee creating Glenda , the Plan 9 mascot, a distant cousin of the WFMU gopher.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When we started the Go project we needed a logo, and Renee volunteered to draw it. It was featured on the first Go T-shirt and the Google Code site.
sourceRefs.textQuote: When we started the Go project we needed a logo, and Renee volunteered to draw it. It was featured on the first Go T-shirt and the Google Code site.

highSignalBlocks.text: When we started the Go project we needed a logo, and Renee volunteered to draw it. It was featured on the first Go T-shirt and the Google Code site.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For the open source launch in 2009, Renee suggested adapting the WFMU gopher as a mascot. And the Go gopher was born:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For the open source launch in 2009, Renee suggested adapting the WFMU gopher as a mascot. And the Go gopher was born:

highSignalBlocks.text: For the open source launch in 2009, Renee suggested adapting the WFMU gopher as a mascot. And the Go gopher was born:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (The gopher has no name, and is called just the “Go gopher”.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (The gopher has no name, and is called just the “Go gopher”.)

highSignalBlocks.text: (The gopher has no name, and is called just the “Go gopher”.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For the launch of the Go App Engine runtime at Google I/O 2011 we engaged Squishable to manufacture the plush gophers. This was the first time the gopher was colored blue and appeared in three dimensions. The first prototype was kinda hairy:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For the launch of the Go App Engine runtime at Google I/O 2011 we engaged Squishable to manufacture the plush gophers. This was the first time the gopher was colored blue and appeared in three dimensions. The first proto

highSignalBlocks.text: For the launch of the Go App Engine runtime at Google I/O 2011 we engaged Squishable to manufacture the plush gophers. This was the first time the gopher was colored blue and appeared in three dimensions. The first prototype was kinda hairy:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: But the second one was just right:
sourceRefs.textQuote: But the second one was just right:

highSignalBlocks.text: But the second one was just right:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Around the same time, Renee roughed out a gopher in clay. This inspired a refined sculpture that became a vinyl figurine made by Kidrobot . The vinyls were first distributed at OSCON 2011.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Around the same time, Renee roughed out a gopher in clay. This inspired a refined sculpture that became a vinyl figurine made by Kidrobot . The vinyls were first distributed at OSCON 2011.

highSignalBlocks.text: Around the same time, Renee roughed out a gopher in clay. This inspired a refined sculpture that became a vinyl figurine made by Kidrobot . The vinyls were first distributed at OSCON 2011.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The gopher therefore exists in many forms, but has always been Renee’s creation. It stands for the Go project and Go programmers everywhere, and is one of the most popular things in the Go world.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The gopher therefore exists in many forms, but has always been Renee’s creation. It stands for the Go project and Go programmers everywhere, and is one of the most popular things in the Go world.

highSignalBlocks.text: The gopher therefore exists in many forms, but has always been Renee’s creation. It stands for the Go project and Go programmers everywhere, and is one of the most popular things in the Go world.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go gopher is a character; a unique creation. Not any old gopher, just as Snoopy is not any old cartoon dog.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go gopher is a character; a unique creation. Not any old gopher, just as Snoopy is not any old cartoon dog.

highSignalBlocks.text: The Go gopher is a character; a unique creation. Not any old gopher, just as Snoopy is not any old cartoon dog.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The gopher images are Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 licensed. That means you can play with the images but you must give credit to their creator (Renee French) wherever they are used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The gopher images are Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 licensed. That means you can play with the images but you must give credit to their creator (Renee French) wherever they are used.

highSignalBlocks.text: The gopher images are Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 licensed. That means you can play with the images but you must give credit to their creator (Renee French) wherever they are used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here are a few gopher adaptations that people have used as mascots for user group mascots and similar organizations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here are a few gopher adaptations that people have used as mascots for user group mascots and similar organizations.

highSignalBlocks.text: Here are a few gopher adaptations that people have used as mascots for user group mascots and similar organizations.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: They’re cute and we like them, but by the Creative Commons rules the groups should give Renee credit, perhaps as a mention on the user group web site.
sourceRefs.textQuote: They’re cute and we like them, but by the Creative Commons rules the groups should give Renee credit, perhaps as a mention on the user group web site.

highSignalBlocks.text: They’re cute and we like them, but by the Creative Commons rules the groups should give Renee credit, perhaps as a mention on the user group web site.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Perhaps the best way to get a gopher is to catch one in the wild at a Go conference. There are two big chances this year: GopherCon (Denver, April 24-26) and dotGo (Paris, October 10).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Perhaps the best way to get a gopher is to catch one in the wild at a Go conference. There are two big chances this year: GopherCon (Denver, April 24-26) and dotGo (Paris, October 10).

highSignalBlocks.text: Perhaps the best way to get a gopher is to catch one in the wild at a Go conference. There are two big chances this year: GopherCon (Denver, April 24-26) and dotGo (Paris, October 10).
